title: Tomato Sauce on Polyester
timeEstimate: 20–60 minutes; oleophilic 2 Dawn passes + sunlight finisher; honest cap on dyed
stainChemistry: Tomato sauce on polyester is a tougher version of the linen / cotton case. Three components: tomato red pigment (lycopene, fat-soluble), plant tannin, and oil. Polyester adds OLEOPHILIC affinity (grabs the oil tight) and heat-sensitivity (hot water sets tannin and lycopene into the fiber, hot dryer locks the orange shadow permanently and yellows polyester over time). The oil-strip step is even more important on polyester than on cotton — Dawn × 2 typical. Recurring traps: hot water (sets), alkaline cleaners (darken tannin permanently), and skipping the oil-strip (orange ghost remains). Honest cap on dyed polyester: faint orange shadow often plateaus.
whyThisWorks: Powder preload pulls bulk before water spreads it. Generous Dawn × 2 strips the oil that polyester grabbed onto and that's holding lycopene in place. Vinegar disrupts tannin grip. 3% peroxide on white polyester partially oxidizes lycopene. Sunlight is the most reliable finisher — UV photodegrades lycopene over hours-to-days. Cool wash plus AIR-DRY locks nothing.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): tomato sauce is lycopene (fat-soluble carotenoid) + tannin polyphenols + olive/canola oil. Polyester is hydrophobic and oleophilic — lycopene partitions strongly into the fiber via the oil carrier. Pro uses NSD oil-strip + acid-side tannin spotter + 3% H₂O₂ + UV-assist + acetic acid neutralizing rinse.
defaultAssumption: Pasta night spill on a polyester shirt. Fresh = real shot. Hot-dried = often a permanent orange shadow. Performance / specialty / dry-clean-only polyester = pro.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Scrape solid sauce off with plastic spoon edge — never rub. Cover spot with cornstarch / baking soda. 20–30 min absorb. Soft-brush off.
Generous Dawn directly. Soft toothbrush 10 min. Polyester is oleophilic — extended dwell. Cool damp-cloth rinse.
Touch with fingertip. Oily feel = repeat Dawn. 2 Dawn passes typical on polyester for tomato oil.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 1:1 white vinegar + cool water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Vinegar + cool water on damp cloth, 5 min. Damp-cloth rinse THOROUGHLY before any peroxide.
WHITE polyester only. Hidden test on dyed. 3% peroxide damp cloth 5–10 min. NEVER mix with chlorine, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners.
Hang in direct sunlight while damp. UV breaks down residual lycopene — most reliable finisher. Multi-day for stubborn orange ghost.
Cool machine wash. AIR-DRY only. Inspect dry. NEVER hot dryer until cleared. Honest cap on dyed polyester: faint orange shadow may remain.

steps: 1. Scrape solids + cornstarch / baking soda preload 20–30 min, soft-brush off.
2. Generous Dawn pretreat, cool, 10 min, soft toothbrush.
3. Repeat Dawn pass — polyester needs 2 typically.
4. White vinegar 1:1 cool 5 min — rinse before peroxide.
5. 3% H₂O₂ for white polyester, hidden test on dyed.
6. Sunlight finisher — UV photodegrades residual orange.
7. Cool wash + AIR-DRY. Honest cap on dyed.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat sets tannin and lycopene into polyester
warnings: Oil-strip BEFORE peroxide — Dawn × 2 typical on polyester
NEVER hot water — sets tannin and lycopene into polyester
NEVER alkaline cleaners — DARKEN tannin permanently
Hidden test on dyed polyester before peroxide
Honest cap on dyed polyester for residual orange

homeSolutions.agent: Polyester Loves Tomato Oil — Powder Plus Two Dawn Passes Plus Sunlight
homeSolutions.instruction: Tomato sauce on polyester is harder than on cotton because polyester is oleophilic (grabs the oil tight) and locks color under heat. The lycopene is fat-soluble — locked behind the oil until Dawn strips it. Path: scrape + powder → Dawn × 2 → vinegar → 3% peroxide on white → sunlight → cool wash. Three hard rules: NO hot water (sets tannin and lycopene), NO alkaline cleaners (darken tannin permanently), NO hot dryer until cleared (locks orange shadow + yellows polyester). Honest cap on dyed polyester — faint orange shadow may persist.
Scrape solid sauce off with a plastic spoon edge — flat-edge work only, never rub. Cover the spot with cornstarch or baking soda. 20 to 30 minutes. Soft-brush off.

Generous Dawn dish soap directly on the spot. Work in with a soft toothbrush for 10 minutes. Polyester is oleophilic — extended dwell. This step is MANDATORY — without oil-strip, peroxide can't reach lycopene. Cool damp-cloth rinse.

homeSolutions.agent: Repeat Dawn — Polyester Needs Two Passes
homeSolutions.instruction: Touch the spot with a clean fingertip. Still oily? Repeat Dawn. Polyester typically needs 2 Dawn passes for tomato oil.
Mix 1 part white vinegar with 1 part cool water. Apply with a damp cloth, press 5 minutes. Disrupts tannin grip. Damp-cloth rinse THOROUGHLY before any peroxide.
WHITE polyester only: 3% drugstore peroxide on a damp cloth, press 5 to 10 minutes for residual lycopene color. Hidden-area test on dyed polyester first.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners — toxic fumes.

homeSolutions.instruction: Hang the damp polyester in direct sunlight for several hours. UV breaks down residual lycopene — most reliable finisher. Multi-day for stubborn orange ghost.
Cool machine wash. AIR-DRY only. Inspect dry. NEVER hot dryer until cleared — locks orange shadow into polyester and yellows fiber over time. Honest cap on dyed polyester: faint shadow may remain.

steps: 1. NO hot water. NO alkaline. NO hot dryer until cleared.
3. Generous Dawn pretreat, cool, 10 min, soft toothbrush.
5. Diluted white vinegar 5 min — rinse before peroxide.
6. 3% peroxide damp cloth on white polyester only.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if hot water or hot dryer was already used (often permanent on polyester). Stop if alkaline cleaner was used as first step. Stop on performance / specialty polyester. Stop after multi-day sunlight plateau on dyed.
warnings: NEVER hot water — sets tannin and lycopene
NEVER alkaline cleaners — DARKEN tannin permanently
Honest cap on dyed for residual orange

neverDo: Never hot water on tomato sauce + polyester
Never skip Dawn oil-strip before peroxide
Never mix peroxide + chlorine + ammonia + alkaline
fiberSensitivities: Polyester is oleophilic — needs 2 Dawn passes for tomato oil
Dyed polyester: hidden test + honest cap

materialWarnings: Polyester loves tomato oil — Dawn × 2 mandatory
Sunlight is the most reliable finisher for residual orange
No hot water / no hot dryer — polyester locks color

escalation.when: Hot water or hot dryer already used. Alkaline cleaner used as first step. Performance / specialty polyester. Multi-day sunlight plateau on dyed.
escalation.whatToTell: "Tomato sauce on [white / dyed / athletic / specialty] polyester, [marinara / vodka / pizza / cream-based], age [fresh / hours / hot-dried suspected], home-treated with [scrape + cornstarch + Dawn × 2 + vinegar + 3% peroxide on white + sunlight × N hours/days + cool wash]. No hot, no alkaline. [Residual orange / hot-dried suspected]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with combination-stain chemistry

contextQuestions: Marinara, vodka sauce, pizza sauce, cream-based?
White, dyed, athletic, or specialty polyester?
Has alkaline cleaner been used as first step?
Has hot water or hot dryer been used?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Cream-based tomato sauces add the milk-protein step (use coffee-cream-polyester order). Polyester loves tomato oil — Dawn × 2 mandatory. Sunlight is the most reliable finisher. Honest cap on dyed polyester for residual orange shadow.
